There's absolutely no shortage of opportunity in America. No. No. I mean, the guy we talked about in the book with the trash company, I mean, the whole business is based on coming to people's garages and taking trash out and charging them for it. I mean, there's no, I mean, so who couldn't do that? I mean, well, I mean seriously. So, okay, there's some stuff you and I do that arguably somebody else couldn't do. At least not instantly. There's nothing I do that somebody else couldn't do if they prepared themselves to do it. I don't know about you, but there's certainly nothing I do that they couldn't, and the reason I know that for a fact is because everything I do or have ever done that has made me a great deal of money or given me a great deal of satisfaction, I sucked at. It was horrible. So it's not like, so if I started out horrible and got good at it to the point that people are willing to give me a lot of money for it, so could the person busing these tables. And presumably, yes, maybe take the same amount of time that took me, maybe not, because there's less barriers and more tools and equipment and all that than what I got good, but even if it took them the same amount of time. So what? Right? The same amount of time if they don't do anything, they'll go be poor, when they're poor now. So the reason that he or she has a low income and you have a high income, there's only one reason. You're superior to them in many ways, including choice. And that's what entitles you to all that you can attract and all that you can take. And as long as you're not taking it by theft. And that there is in this life karma for most of that. Sometimes it's slow, but you know, I mean, Bernie made off 30 years. That's slow. The wheels of justice grind slowly, but in one way, shape or form, they usually do catch up. And if he dies relatively young, then maybe he won't have paid such a terrible price, but if he happens to live to be 80 or 90, then this is not going to be a real good deal. But so setting aside criminality, and I mean outright criminality, because the line needs to be pretty black and white, not gray, then I think to be at this level, you really have to have what Carnegie described. You have to have a profound sense of superiority over most of the other people you see around you. And that's difficult for a lot of people to like conceptually and philosophically and emotionally get over, because in many cases you've been taught not to have that. You've been taught not to have your entire life by parents, family, society, whatever religion you belong to, whatever it's all taught you that this is like wrong. And I don't think it's either right or wrong. I think it's just reality and then it's necessity. So if you start to try and deal with this, then there's a couple things that are true. So on 170, one of the things that's true is, it means you have to find or invent ways to actually make yourself superior to others. So that your belief in your superiority is grounded on something other than arrogance. Now, since most people don't think much, don't do much, it's not really hard to accomplish this feat. This is not really that big of a hurdle to get over in your community, in your wherever, in your industry, in your profession, in your, I mean, look, the, what does the average dentist make? 180,000. That's a big number. What's the average guy in the home improvement business make? Average guy. 60,000. What's the average life coach, strategic counselor? Yeah, the answer is zero. That was actually the correct answer.
